refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating mission context into performance targets. This remains solution-neutral and avoids prescribing 'method,' 'document,' or 'history' which are design choices.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints from stakeholders), the system produces a traceable set of performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy those inputs. Verify traceability from worksite inputs to derived requirements.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall capture worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements (e.g., cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from captured worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to source mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a mission-context capture module that accepts worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental conditions) and applies a deterministic mapping function to derive performance targets; maintain a versioned baseline registry with change history and traceability links.

DP-B: Establish a parameterized performance-target template library indexed by worksite classification; populate templates via structured intake of mission context; log all baseline updates with timestamp, rationale, and operator identity.

DP-C: Deploy a context-aware inference engine that ingests worksite metadata (mission type, hazard profile, operational constraints) and outputs performance requirements via rule-based or model-driven synthesis; persist all derivation artifacts and audit trails.

DP-D: Create a federated requirement-derivation service that decouples mission-context ingestion (input interface) from performance-target synthesis (logic) from baseline persistence (storage); enable independent evolution of each concern.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Create a federated requirement-derivation service that decouples mission-context ingestion (input interface) from performance-target synthesis (logic) from baseline persistence (storage); enable independent evolution of each concern.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between context ingestion and synthesis logic (mitigated by explicit service boundary in DP-D).
Bidirectional dependency between baseline persistence and traceability queries (managed via event-sourced or append-only audit log).
Implicit assumption that worksite safety plan and operational policies are machine-readable or require human interpretation (risk: requires clarification in IM layer).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D achieves low coupling by separating concerns: (1) input validation/normalization, (2) derivation logic, (3) persistence and history. This allows independent testing, versioning, and scaling. DP-A and DP-B are more monolithic and risk tight coupling between mapping logic and registry. DP-C introduces inference complexity that may obscure traceability unless carefully instrumented.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is available in structured or semi-structured form (safety plan, policies). RISK: If inputs are unstructured (e.g., free-text documents), derivation logic becomes fragile. MITIGATION: Define input schema and validation rules in IM layer.

ASSUMPTION: Performance targets can be deterministically derived from mission context without human judgment. RISK: Safety-critical systems often require expert review and sign-off. MITIGATION: DP-D should include a review/approval gate in the synthesis workflow.

ASSUMPTION: Baseline history and traceability are sufficient for compliance. RISK: Regulatory bodies may require formal change-control and impact-analysis processes. MITIGATION: Extend DP-D to include change-impact assessment and stakeholder notification.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite inputs remain stable during a mission cycle. RISK: Dynamic operational changes (e.g., hazard escalation, resource constraints) may invalidate derived targets mid-mission. MITIGATION: Implement continuous context monitoring and re-derivation triggers.

ASSUMPTION: The system has authority to modify performance targets based on mission context. RISK: Organizational governance may require centralized approval. MITIGATION: Clarify decision authority in acceptance criteria and IM layer.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005 (revised): The NGHE solution shall provide a federated service that (1) ingests worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) via a validated input interface; (2) applies a documented, traceable derivation logic to synthesize performance targets; (3) persists all baselines, calibration updates, and derivation artifacts in an auditable registry with full change history; (4) enables queries linking each performance target to its source mission-context inputs and derivation rationale. Acceptance criteria: (a) All performance targets are traceable to worksite inputs via documented derivation rules. (b) Baseline history includes timestamp, operator identity, change rationale, and impact assessment. (c) System supports re-derivation and comparison of alternative baselines. (d) Derivation logic is testable and version-controlled independently of persistence layer.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Federated requirement-derivation service that ingests mission context, synthesizes performance targets, and persists baseline data with decoupled concerns for independent evolution

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Mission-context ingestion: receives operational state, environmental parameters, and mission objectives to inform requirement derivation

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Performance-target synthesis output: communicates derived performance requirements and baseline targets to vehicle control and teleoperation systems

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Baseline persistence and safety constraint feedback: persists derived baselines and receives safety-critical constraint updates that influence requirement re-derivation

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of requirement derivation tied to performance baselines and safety constraints. Mission-context ingestion (Telemetry) and performance-target synthesis (Tasking) are well-scoped inputs/outputs. Baseline persistence aligns with safety-critical data stewardship. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation may need read-only access to derived baselines, but this is manageable via SafetyAlert interface.

Moderate fit: NGHE Solution is a broad system-level container. Requirement derivation could reside here, but it would blur the boundary between system orchestration and safety-critical logic. High diffusion risk: Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services would all need direct access to derivation logic, creating tight coupling.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a platform/hardware abstraction. Requirement derivation is a cross-cutting concern that must inform Vehicle behavior but should not be owned by it. High diffusion: Mission context, performance synthesis, and baseline persistence would leak across Safety Services and Teleoperation.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is an operator-interaction domain. While it consumes derived performance targets, it should not own the derivation logic itself. High diffusion: Mission-context ingestion and baseline persistence belong in safety/autonomy layers, not operator interface.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most cohesive boundary because (1) requirement derivation is fundamentally a safety-critical function that must enforce baseline constraints, (2) mission-context ingestion via Telemetry is a natural input to safety reasoning, (3) performance-target synthesis via Tasking is a safety-informed output, and (4) baseline persistence is a core safety-stewardship responsibility. The federated architecture (decoupled input, logic, storage) can be realized as internal sub-modules within Safety Services without diffusing across Vehicle or Teleoperation.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation may require real-time read access to derived baselines, creating latency-sensitive coupling via SafetyAlert interface. Mitigation: Design SafetyAlert as a publish-subscribe pattern with cached baseline snapshots.

Risk: Mission-context ingestion (Telemetry) may carry domain-specific payloads that Safety Services does not naturally interpret (e.g., vehicle-specific sensor calibration). Mitigation: Define a canonical mission-context schema at the Telemetry interface; Safety Services consumes only schema-compliant fields.

Risk: Baseline persistence may grow to include non-safety metadata (e.g., performance tuning history), blurring Safety Services' scope. Mitigation: Enforce strict data governance: Safety Services persists only safety-critical baselines; performance history is owned by Vehicle or NGHE Solution.

Risk: If requirement derivation logic becomes highly specialized per vehicle type, Safety Services may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Design derivation as a pluggable strategy pattern; Safety Services orchestrates but does not hard-code vehicle-specific rules.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP-D is well-scoped and does not require revision. However, to strengthen containment, explicitly decompose the federated service into three internal sub-modules within Safety Services: (1) MissionContextAdapter (ingests Telemetry, normalizes to canonical schema), (2) RequirementDerivationEngine (synthesizes performance targets, emits via Tasking), (3) BaselineStore (persists and retrieves baselines, interfaces with SafetyAlert). This internal decomposition ensures the DP's three concerns (input, logic, storage) remain decoupled while the IM boundary (Safety Services) remains cohesive and prevents diffusion across Vehicle and Teleoperation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D correctly maps to FR CR-NGHE-005. The federated requirement-derivation service (DP) directly addresses the functional requirement to derive performance requirements from worksite mission context. The three decoupled concerns (mission-context ingestion, performance-target synthesis, baseline persistence) are logically sound and enable independent evolution. The DP is expressed at the appropriate level of abstraction for a design parameter and does not prematurely constrain implementation.

diffusion_risks: Latency-sensitive coupling risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation may require real-time read access to derived baselines via SafetyAlert interface. If not carefully managed via publish-subscribe caching, this could create tight temporal coupling and degrade performance. Mitigation: Implement SafetyAlert as an event-driven pub-sub pattern with baseline snapshot caching to decouple producers from consumers.

Mission-context schema interpretation risk: Telemetry payloads may carry vehicle-specific or domain-specific metadata (e.g., sensor calibration, environmental sensor types) that Safety Services does not naturally interpret. If Safety Services must hard-code interpretation logic for each vehicle variant, diffusion occurs. Mitigation: Define and enforce a canonical mission-context schema at the Telemetry interface boundary; Safety Services consumes only schema-compliant fields and delegates vehicle-specific interpretation to adapters.

Scope creep in baseline persistence: Over time, baseline persistence may accumulate non-safety metadata (performance tuning history, calibration drift logs, operator preferences) that blur Safety Services' ownership boundary. Mitigation: Establish strict data governance: Safety Services owns only safety-critical baselines; performance history and tuning metadata are owned by Vehicle or NGHE Solution and accessed via read-only SafetyAlert queries.

Vehicle-type specialization bottleneck: If requirement derivation logic becomes highly specialized per vehicle type or mission class, Safety Services may become a bottleneck or require vehicle-specific branching logic. Mitigation: Design derivation engine as a pluggable strategy pattern; Safety Services orchestrates derivation strategies but does not hard-code vehicle-specific rules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the correct IM boundary (containment score 0.85, diffusion score 0.15). The federated architecture is well-aligned with Safety Services' responsibility for safety-critical logic and baseline stewardship. The three external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert inout) are clean and minimize diffusion. To strengthen containment and reduce the 0.15 diffusion risk, decompose the federated service into three internal sub-modules: (1) MissionContextAdapter (normalizes Telemetry inputs to canonical schema), (2) RequirementDerivationEngine (synthesizes performance targets, emits via Tasking), (3) BaselineStore (persists and retrieves baselines, interfaces with SafetyAlert). This internal decomposition ensures the DP's three concerns remain decoupled while the IM boundary remains cohesive and prevents leakage into Vehicle or Teleoperation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criteria require (1) documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs, and (2) history of baselines/calibration updates. Both are directly realizable within Safety Services: RequirementDerivationEngine produces traceable targets (with audit trail linking each target to input mission-context fields), and BaselineStore maintains versioned baseline history. Traceability can be verified via query of SafetyAlert interface and baseline persistence logs. Compliance with worksite safety plan and operational policies can be verified by inspecting the derivation rules embedded in RequirementDerivationEngine and the constraints enforced by BaselineStore.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR CR-NGHE-005 is allocated entirely to the Safety Services IM boundary via DP-D. The allocation is unambiguous: Safety Services owns requirement derivation, mission-context ingestion, performance-target synthesis, and baseline persistence. External consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation) interact with Safety Services only via the three defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert), eliminating allocation ambiguity. No shared ownership or cross-cutting allocation is present.

recommended_revisions: Decompose DP-D's federated service into three explicit internal sub-modules within Safety Services: (1) MissionContextAdapter (ingests Telemetry, normalizes to canonical mission-context schema, validates against worksite safety plan), (2) RequirementDerivationEngine (applies derivation rules to normalized context, synthesizes performance targets with full audit trail, emits via Tasking), (3) BaselineStore (persists derived baselines with version history, enforces safety constraints, interfaces with SafetyAlert for baseline updates and read-only queries). This decomposition is internal to Safety Services and does not change the DP or IM boundary; it strengthens containment by making the three decoupled concerns explicit and preventing diffusion.

Define a canonical mission-context schema at the Telemetry interface. This schema must include: (a) worksite identifier and safety plan reference, (b) operational state (e.g., mission phase, vehicle mode), (c) environmental parameters (e.g., terrain, weather, visibility), (d) mission objectives (e.g., task type, priority, constraints). Safety Services consumes only schema-compliant fields; vehicle-specific interpretation is delegated to MissionContextAdapter adapters. Document the schema in the interface specification and enforce validation at the Telemetry boundary.

Implement SafetyAlert as a publish-subscribe event stream with baseline snapshot caching. Vehicle and Teleoperation subscribe to baseline-update events; Safety Services publishes snapshots at defined intervals or on significant changes. This decouples real-time consumers from the BaselineStore and reduces latency-sensitive coupling. Document the event schema and subscription protocol in the SafetyAlert interface specification.

Establish data governance rules for baseline persistence: (1) Safety Services owns only safety-critical baselines (e.g., performance limits, constraint thresholds, calibration offsets tied to safety). (2) Performance tuning history, operator preferences, and non-safety metadata are owned by Vehicle or NGHE Solution and accessed via read-only SafetyAlert queries. (3) Document the boundary between safety-critical and non-safety metadata in the BaselineStore specification.

Design RequirementDerivationEngine as a pluggable strategy pattern. Define a derivation-strategy interface that encapsulates vehicle-type-specific or mission-class-specific logic. Safety Services orchestrates strategy selection and invocation but does not hard-code vehicle-specific rules. This prevents Safety Services from becoming a bottleneck and allows independent evolution of vehicle-specific derivation logic.

Add traceability metadata to each derived performance target: (a) source mission-context fields that influenced the target, (b) derivation rule(s) applied, (c) timestamp and baseline version, (d) worksite safety plan reference. This metadata must be persisted in BaselineStore and queryable via SafetyAlert to satisfy the acceptance criterion 'performance targets traceable to worksite inputs.'

Document the baseline history retention policy: (a) how long baselines are retained, (b) how calibration updates are versioned and archived, (c) how historical baselines are accessed for audit or rollback. This satisfies the acceptance criterion 'maintains a history of baselines/calibration updates.'

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the DP-IM gate explicitly recommends internal decomposition of DP-D's federated service into three decoupled sub-concerns (MissionContextAdapter, RequirementDerivationEngine, BaselineStore) to strengthen containment and reduce diffusion risk from 0.15 to near-zero. This decomposition is internal to the Safety Services IM boundary and does not alter the DP or IM allocation, but it requires explicit sub-level FRs to drive the design of these three internal modules. Given high safety criticality and the identified latency-sensitive coupling, mission-context schema interpretation, and scope-creep risks, deriving next-level sub-FRs is justified to ensure each internal concern is independently verifiable and maintainable. The recommended revisions are architectural in nature and require functional decomposition to be realized.
next_level_sub_frs: FR CR-NGHE-005.1: Normalize and validate mission-context telemetry payloads against a canonical schema that includes worksite identifier, operational state, environmental parameters, and mission objectives, rejecting non-compliant inputs and delegating vehicle-specific interpretation to adapters.

FR CR-NGHE-005.2: Synthesize performance targets from normalized mission-context by applying derivation rules with full audit trail (source fields, rules applied, timestamp, baseline version, worksite safety plan reference), emitting targets via Tasking interface with traceability metadata.

FR CR-NGHE-005.3: Persist derived baselines with version history, enforce safety-critical data governance (Safety Services owns only safety-critical baselines; non-safety metadata is owned externally), and provide read-only query access via SafetyAlert interface with baseline snapshot caching to decouple real-time consumers.

revise.why: DP-D should be refined to explicitly name the three internal sub-DPs corresponding to the three sub-FRs: (1) DP-D.1: Canonical mission-context schema with validation adapter pattern; (2) DP-D.2: Pluggable derivation-strategy engine with audit-trail logging; (3) DP-D.3: Versioned baseline store with publish-subscribe event caching and data-governance enforcement. This refinement makes the federated service's internal structure explicit and traceable to the recommended revisions, reducing ambiguity for implementation teams.

assumptions: Mission-context telemetry payloads are vehicle-specific and require normalization to a canonical schema before derivation logic can be applied safely.
Performance target derivation is deterministic and rule-based, allowing audit trails to be reconstructed from stored rules and input context.
Real-time consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation) can tolerate eventual-consistency baseline snapshots via pub-sub caching rather than synchronous reads from BaselineStore.
Safety-critical baselines can be cleanly separated from performance-tuning metadata and non-safety operational history.
Derivation logic can be encapsulated as pluggable strategies without requiring hard-coded vehicle-type branching in Safety Services.
open_risks: Latency-sensitive coupling: If SafetyAlert pub-sub caching is not implemented with sufficient snapshot frequency or cache invalidation, Vehicle and Teleoperation may operate on stale baselines, creating safety hazard. Mitigation: Define snapshot SLA (e.g., max 100ms staleness) and implement cache invalidation on significant baseline changes.

Mission-context schema evolution: If worksite safety plans or vehicle types introduce new mission-context fields not covered by the canonical schema, adapters may silently drop critical information. Mitigation: Implement strict schema versioning and adapter validation; log dropped fields as warnings.

Derivation-strategy bottleneck: If strategy selection logic becomes complex (e.g., multi-vehicle, multi-mission-class combinations), Safety Services may become a bottleneck or require frequent updates. Mitigation: Design strategy registry as a plugin system with lazy loading; allow strategies to be registered at runtime.

Baseline persistence scope creep: Over time, non-safety metadata may accumulate in BaselineStore if data-governance rules are not enforced at write time. Mitigation: Implement write-time validation in BaselineStore to reject non-safety-critical fields; document ownership boundaries in interface specification.

Traceability metadata overhead: Storing full audit trails for every derived target may create storage and query-performance overhead. Mitigation: Implement tiered retention (e.g., detailed audit for 30 days, summary for 1 year) and index traceability fields for efficient querying.
evidence_needed: Canonical mission-context schema specification with worksite, operational state, environmental, and mission-objective fields; validation rules; and vehicle-specific adapter interface definition.
Derivation-rule specification with examples showing how mission-context inputs map to performance targets; audit-trail format and retention policy.
SafetyAlert pub-sub event schema and snapshot caching strategy; SLA for baseline freshness; cache invalidation triggers.
Data-governance policy document defining safety-critical vs. non-safety-critical baseline fields; ownership boundaries; read-only query constraints.
Derivation-strategy interface specification and plugin-registry design; examples of vehicle-type-specific and mission-class-specific strategies.
BaselineStore schema with version-history structure, traceability-metadata fields, and write-time validation rules.

Acceptance-test plan for each sub-FR, including traceability verification (query SafetyAlert for target-to-context mapping), baseline-history verification (query BaselineStore for versioned snapshots), and schema-compliance verification (validate Telemetry inputs against canonical schema).
